description: Monday 15 December | Social Services and Community Sub-Committee A Redress System for Abuse in Care Bill Hearing of evidence: Te Iwi Preston Kay Jones Ben Keith Dr Debbie Hager Royal Commission Forum Karl Tauri Dr Rawiri Waretini-Karena Kenneth Clearwater ONZM The Law Association of New Zealand Hannah Kim, Committee Co-Convenor, Parole and Prisoner Rights Law Committee Kate MacKay, Committee Member, Mental Health and Disability Law Committee Aaron Douglas Andrew Goddard
descriptionSummary: A. Redress System for Abuse in Care Bill. Te Iwi Preston

Um, survivor support must be accessible, trauma-informed and culturally safe mandate, accessible trauma-informed support delivered by a workforce trained in tikanga Maori cultural safety and trauma-informed practice is important.

This bill fails survivors by not guaranteeing the quality or cultural competence of the support they receive. Survivors cannot be expected to reenter unsafe systems or educate those meant to help them without mandatory training. In tikanga, Maori and trauma-informed practice redress risks.

Retraumatization rather than healing support must meet survivors where they are not where the system is comfortable. Redress must also be independent survivor led and Maori governed establish a dedicated independence, uh, uh, statutory redress agency and governance led by survivors and UA redress cannot be credible if it remains controlled by the same system that caused harm.

Independence is not optional. It is foundational to trust. Survivors in UA must sit at the governance table, not as advisors, but as decision makers. Without this, the bill risks becoming another administrative exercise rather than genuine pathway to justice and accountability.

Finally, Maori LED pathways must address intergenerational harm, prioritize Maori led healing pathways that recognize and respond to inter intergenerational trauma affecting whanau, ana and descendants. Abuse and state care does not end with the survivor.

It ripples through generations. This bill narrowly frame frames harm as individual and historic for when, when for Maori, it is collective and ongoing healing must be whanau centered and centered, grounded in reconnection, restoration and Maori defined wellbeing.

Okay. We have five minutes with you this afternoon. The time is yours, Kira. Um, as you know, my name is Kay Jones. I'm fifth generation, um, and Phan, new Zealander and third generation Wellingtonian. I'm not a lawyer, although I have drafted law.

I'm not a survivor of care, although I have friends who are, and I have attended Whoi and have heard directly from people who have survived and who have been looking forward to getting some form of compensation to help 'em deal with the health issues that they're still experiencing from years of abuse, including rape by people within state institutions.

The reason that I'm here today is in part because my grandmother was an elder in her church, and she said, and she taught us, if we see something bad happening, speak up. Even if we can't change it, we have a duty to witness that. So when I see this bill coming here right before Christmas, it reminds me of the grin who stole Christmas, the Christmas Carol, where Ebenezer Scrooge was confronted by ghosts.

And I feel that this is the types of stories that, um, members of parliament need to be reflecting on, perhaps reading Aaron Smails, um, taken by the state account in, um, newsroom this week that came out yesterday. Of course, you will have, I hope, read the full abuse and care document and know the long-term harms that have been caused people.

Now I'm a parent and a former teacher, which means I have been in loco parentis, and that means having responsibility to people in my care. And that care does not disappear just because a class may have ended. The state has been in loco parentis for all of those young people who are now older adults who've been taken into care, abused, and they have a duty of care that they have failed.

The bill tries to say, oh, if you did something years afterwards, you are not, um, going to get compensation for something that happened earlier. Well, it's simply false legal reasoning because each event is separate and the state has got perfect ability to look at those things separately.

The same with the apology. That's a ology if it's not actually going to make any real difference to the people who were harmed. So I'm not going to read much. I would be very happy to answer questions from my years of listening to people, but I don't know that it would make any difference.

So I'm simply here to say in my t-shirt, which is from just speak says, build communities, not prisons. And it's that whole thing. We need to look at the holistic of what can the government do for better, not for multiply harming people, making them angry or despondent or causing suicides or, or whatever.

I mean, like, you have a duty to do better. Thank, thank you. And the way this is set up, a, it is a really bad signal to the people who have been in the system so that some of them will not be in that situation. But I'm sorry, I do not have faith in people getting what they are due in the first instance. It's like, you need to do the, the fixing first before saying, okay, you subsequently acted exactly the same way that maybe the gang who were the only people showing you compassion or support that took you in and encouraged you to act in a certain way. They're trying to go straight to live within their means. Food is hard, everything is hard. The committee has heard from survivors, has heard of obstruction denial outright, um, deceit over the past several decades, and I won't repeat that.

Instead, I'll make three points from a lawyer's perspective. First is that redress requires a robust approach. Supporters of the bill have made reference to the Australian and Scottish schemes, and those are very good solid statutes. This bill is not, it does not create a statutory reds scheme.

Instead, it relies upon an ad hoc mishmash of schemes, and I don't use those words lightly. Mm-hmm. Second point is to acknowledge the Lake Alice scheme, which I had the privilege to work with alongside Ms. McEnroe and others. That scheme is not perfect, but the responsible minister of the Honor, Erica Stanford and Paul Davison, Casey, who came in as arbiter, both gave it their absolute best I can say.

One particular point I make for the purpose of the bill though, is that scheme was informed by independent expert legal advice about the crown's redress obligations. And I'll come to those. This bill does not have any trace of that. There is no reference in the policy papers, no reference in any of the provisions.

And the written submission sets out what those redress obligations are to compensate, rehabilitate, punish those responsible. The bill does none of those things. Third, three comments about the bill As it stands, one, it continues that ad hoc approach. And despite the models from other jurisdictions, we have nothing here.

There's the Crown response office. The policy agency has said there is further work to be done to mid 2026. Meanwhile, abuse survivors become older frailer, and are still waiting for New Zealand to follow other jurisdictions that have found this possible. The difficulty is simply in taking up this challenge, and I urge the committee to do that.

Second, in the mo I think the most, uh, controversial part of the bill is constraining compensation for individual offenders. I can really only add a couple of perspectives. I've dealt with the worst criminal offenders in the country. Professionally, I can say I've also dealt with a great deal of abuse and care. The suggestion that one negates the other is not one that a fair-minded person who knew that detail, who'd seen that experience, uh, can consider.

And the simple point is this, whether for an international lawyer, whether for a black law letter lawyer where there is a wrong, the wrongdoer must remedy, the crown must remedy. There may be a private issue between a criminal offender and his or her victim, but that does not excuse the crown and can't point to something else. And my last point about that, the committee will know well, just how much of a bureaucracy, how much money has gone into the architecture of redress?

The $774 million announced by the government earlier this year to its credit, more than two thirds goes to administrative overheads, creating yet another office, yet another process does very little. My third point is about the final clause of the bill, which says that apologies are not legally actionable or not not legally relevant.

Apologies, as you all know, are a critical part of redress. We all heard the Prime Minister's very solemn apology last year to say that those are not legally valid, not legally relevant. We have to address both sides of that coin saying that we, we don't address this because it might offend the other is, is an easy out. It, it doesn't follow one, one doesn't compute into the other. Thank you, appreciate, appreciate making your submission most.

So myself and Rosalyn and I, uh, we're gonna speak on behalf of the Royal Commission Forum. Now, as you know, our submission strongly opposes this bill.

The bill is discriminatory. It's unfair, it'll be costly, and it fails to meet the demands for justice that were endorsed by this government during the parliamentary apology on 12 November, 2024. You know, these are discriminatory components and there are other ways in which we could do serve the same outcomes. I'd refer the committee to the UN basic principles and guidelines on the right to remedy and reparations per victims of gross violations of international human rights. Those 2006 guidelines are the leading standard for international law in this area. And they require that redress policies be without quoting, now be without discrimination of any kind or any ground without exception, not sometime exceptions without exception.

It's unclear why survivors being singled out like this. Those provisions will also have a chilling effect on applicants and applications creating more difficulties and uncertainties for survivors. My research into redress program shows that as administrative complexity increases the slower these programs operate and the more they cost to administer.

Sure, no, thanks for that clarification. Um, the, uh, question, Thank you, Mr. Chair, and thank you for your submission. Uh, do you believe that excluding survivors of abuse and care from accessing redress because of crimes they've been sentenced for as a form of double jeopardy?
